skill.name: clinical-decision-support
skill.description: Generate clinical decision support documents for research and pharmaceutical teams, including biomarker-stratified cohort analyses, evidence-graded treatment recommendations, statistical summaries, and publication-ready LaTeX reports.
skill.summary: Create evidence-based clinical decision support reports with biomarker, outcome, and GRADE methodology.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives from clinical terminology, Markdown examples, LaTeX commands, and ordinary Python file handling. The remaining risk is medium because the skill allows Bash, includes executable Python scripts, writes local clinical artifacts, and interpolates user-provided decision text into LaTeX without escaping. No confirmed malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or network code was found.

medium_findings.title: Executable Scripts With Local File Read and Write Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for elevated operational risk, not malware. The skill enables Bash and documents Python analysis scripts that read user-supplied clinical files and write report, table, figure, and validation outputs. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can overwrite local files or process sensitive clinical data if used carelessly. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: Direct script and allowed-tool evidence exists, but reviewed code shows local analysis behavior rather than malicious execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence shows Bash permission and Python scripts that read and write local files. The implementation appears purpose-aligned and lacks shell execution or exfiltration behavior.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped User Text in Generated LaTeX
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for content-injection risk. The decision-tree helper reads text or JSON input and places node text directly inside LaTeX commands without escaping special characters. This can break generated documents and may become dangerous if untrusted LaTeX is compiled in a permissive environment. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The interpolation is visible in the TikZ generation paths, but exploitation depends on later compilation settings and user input trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly embeds user-controlled text into LaTeX output. The risk is contextual because the script does not compile the LaTeX by itself.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Clinical Data Handling Requires User Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for privacy risk in normal use. The skill is designed for patient cohort and clinical outcome data and warns users to remove HIPAA identifiers, while scripts generate CSV, text, TeX, PDF, and PNG artifacts. No exfiltration was found, but users must de-identify inputs and control output locations. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The data domain and file-writing behavior are clear, but the code includes a basic HIPAA identifier checker and does not intentionally collect secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly processes clinical cohort data and writes derived artifacts. The privacy risk is inherent to the workflow, not evidence of malicious handling.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Reviewed representative high-severity hits and found clinical terms, assay names, statistical language, and argument parser text rather than cryptographic functions such as MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines show clinical content or parser setup, and the script search found no cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative static hits are clinical documentation and command-line argument descriptions. No weak cryptographic algorithm implementation was found in reviewed scripts.

low_findings.title: Ruby and Shell Backtick Alerts Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for most reported locations. Static detections point to Markdown code fences, inline file names, dependency examples, and tabular report examples, not Ruby backtick execution. One Bash command example is documentation for running a helper script and is covered as medium operational risk. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The cited locations are rendered documentation blocks and no Ruby or shell backtick execution primitives were found in scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation syntax, not executable Ruby code. The actual scripts do not contain subprocess, os.system, or equivalent shell invocation.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Alerts Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Network reconnaissance hits are clinical references such as ClinicalTrials.gov search terms or outcome-analysis language, and the env_access hit is the term iUPD in a medical response category. No requests, urllib, socket, process environment, or credential access was found. Confidence: 0.9. Confidence reasoning: Representative lines are clinical guidance text, and broad code search found no network client or environment variable access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are medical documentation and search-term placeholders. No code-level network or environment access was identified.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Key File Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported sensitive key location corresponds to iterating over JSON node keys in a decision-tree generator, not certificate, SSH, or private key material. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The reviewed line uses nodes.keys() as a dictionary operation, and no key file contents or certificate material were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is a standard Python dictionary operation. It does not access or embed cryptographic key material.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled LaTeX Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script generates TikZ LaTeX by embedding user-provided algorithm text and JSON node text. Escape LaTeX metacharacters and compile without shell escape when using untrusted inputs. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The string interpolation is explicit, but impact depends on downstream LaTeX compilation behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The generator inserts input text into LaTeX commands without escaping. The script itself only writes a .tex file.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Selected Output Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Several scripts write files to user-selected output paths. This is expected behavior, but users should avoid sensitive directories and review output paths before running commands. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: Multiple script paths write outputs from CLI arguments or defaults, but no stealthy or destructive file behavior was observed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Local writes are explicit and purpose-aligned. The concern is accidental overwrite or data placement, not malicious persistence.

content.value_statement: Clinical research teams need consistent evidence summaries, biomarker analyses, and treatment algorithms. This skill creates structured CDS reports with statistics, GRADE evidence grading, and publication-ready LaTeX formatting.
actual_capabilities: Creates patient cohort analysis documents with biomarker stratification and outcome comparisons.
Builds treatment recommendation reports using GRADE-style evidence levels and recommendation strength.
Generates LaTeX report structures with executive summaries, tables, flowcharts, and references.
Provides Python helpers for biomarker classification, cohort tables, decision trees, survival analysis, and validation checks.
Supports common oncology research metrics including ORR, PFS, OS, hazard ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values.
Includes reference guidance for evidence synthesis, outcome analysis, clinical algorithms, and biomarker classification.
limitations: It is not a substitute for clinician judgment, regulatory review, or institutional medical approval.
Users must provide accurate, current evidence and de-identified clinical data.
Some scripts require optional Python packages such as pandas, scipy, lifelines, matplotlib, or pyyaml.
Generated LaTeX and statistical outputs need human review before publication or clinical use.

use_cases.title: Summarize Biomarker-Stratified Trial Outcomes
use_cases.description: Create a cohort analysis comparing outcomes across biomarker-defined patient groups, with tables and statistical interpretation.

use_cases.title: Draft Evidence-Graded Treatment Guidance
use_cases.description: Prepare a treatment recommendation report that links therapies, biomarkers, evidence quality, and monitoring considerations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Decision Algorithms for Review
use_cases.description: Generate a structured treatment pathway with decision points, supporting evidence, and publication-ready flowchart guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Cohort Summary
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a short structured report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a clinical cohort summary for de-identified patients with disease type, biomarker groups, treatments, response rates, PFS, OS, and key limitations.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Biomarker Analysis Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a biomarker-stratified cohort analysis comparing group outcomes. Include baseline characteristics, ORR, DCR, median PFS, median OS, hazard ratios, p-values, and interpretation.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Treatment Recommendations
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare evidence-based treatment recommendations for the specified disease state. Grade each recommendation using GRADE, cite key trials or guidelines, and include monitoring requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Produce a Full CDS Package
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a complete publication-ready document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a full clinical decision support document with page-one executive summary, evidence review, biomarker-guided algorithm, treatment sequencing, statistical tables, limitations, and review checklist.

output_examples.input: A request to compare 60 HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer patients by hormone receptor status.
output_examples.output: A structured cohort analysis with an executive summary, biomarker subgroup table, response outcomes, survival interpretation, limitations, and clinical implications.

output_examples.input: A request for advanced NSCLC recommendations using PD-L1, EGFR, ALK, and performance status.
output_examples.output: A treatment recommendation report with GRADE ratings, biomarker-directed therapy sequencing, evidence tables, safety monitoring, and a decision pathway.

output_examples.input: A request to validate a draft clinical decision support document before sharing.
output_examples.output: A review summary covering missing sections, citation gaps, statistical reporting issues, possible identifiers, and biomarker nomenclature concerns.

best_practices: Use de-identified inputs and remove HIPAA identifiers before generating or validating documents.
Provide source evidence, assay methods, cut points, follow-up time, and statistical assumptions.
Review all generated recommendations, LaTeX, and figures with qualified clinical and regulatory experts.
anti_patterns: Do not use generated recommendations as direct bedside orders or individual patient treatment plans.
Do not compile untrusted LaTeX with shell escape or permissive file access.
Do not publish outputs without checking citations, statistics, conflicts of interest, and data privacy.

faq.question: Is this skill for individual patient care?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for population-level research, cohort analysis, evidence synthesis, and guideline-style documents.

faq.question: Can it generate statistical tables and survival summaries?
faq.answer: Yes. The included scripts and instructions support cohort tables, survival curves, hazard ratios, p-values, and related summaries.

faq.question: Does it automatically verify current medical guidelines?
faq.answer: No. Users must supply current evidence and have qualified experts verify recommendations before use.

faq.question: Can it handle protected health information?
faq.answer: It should only be used with de-identified data. The skill includes guidance and a basic validator, but privacy review remains the user responsibility.

faq.question: What formats does it target?
faq.answer: It primarily targets structured LaTeX and PDF-style clinical research documents with tables, figures, and executive summaries.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes Bash permission, executable scripts, file writes, and LaTeX generation from user text. No malicious behavior was confirmed.
